summary: DHH celebrates Google Cloud's decision to eliminate egress fees for customers migrating away from GCP. He notes that 37signals faces $300,000-$400,000 in fees just to leave AWS S3, making Google's move significant. He's particularly surprised that the cloud exit discourse, which only gained momentum in 2023, is already influencing a major hyperscaler. While skeptical of Google's altruism, he offers two theories: preempting EU regulation and a competitive strategy to steal marketshare from AWS and Azure. Regardless of motive, he views the move as unambiguously good for cloud competition and cloud exits alike.
key_points: Major cloud providers charge absurd egress fees that effectively trap customers — 37signals faces $300k-$400k just to leave AWS S3
Google Cloud eliminated all data transfer fees for customers migrating away, effective immediately and globally
The cloud exit movement that gained steam in 2023 is already influencing hyperscaler pricing decisions
Google's likely motivations are preempting EU anti-competitive regulation and gaining competitive advantage as the distant third-place cloud provider
Regardless of Google's motives, eliminating egress fees improves both cloud competition and the viability of leaving the cloud entirely
money_quotes: The absurd egress fees charged by major hyperscalers have been one of the biggest impediments for companies to leave the cloud.
The price for taking our data elsewhere is in the crazy-land region of $300,000-$400,000!
I wouldn't have guessed that the cloud exit discourse, which only really gained steam in 2023, would already be influencing one of the three major hyperscalers!
I don't know how many people will actually buy the idea that Google is just doing this out of the goodness of their heart.
Google is a distant third in the cloud wars, and they stand to gain far more from goading competitors into following suit than they'll lose on their end.
But who cares! I commend Google for doing this, regardless of whatever ulterior motives or self-interest is guiding it.
Cutting the egress racket absolutely will improve competition in the cloud and make it easier for companies to leave it entirely.
key_insight: Cloud egress fees function as a data hostage racket, and Google's elimination of them — whatever the motive — validates the cloud exit movement and pressures AWS and Azure to follow suit.
